key_points: Pressure is the force acting per unit area and changes with variation in force or area
Atmospheric pressure results from the weight of air acting on Earth's surface due to gravity
Temperature differences cause unequal air pressure which leads to wind formation
Warm air expands, becomes less dense, and rises creating low-pressure regions
Cooler air moves toward low-pressure regions causing strong winds
Cyclones form when warm moist air rises rapidly over warm ocean waters creating a rotating low-pressure system
The Coriolis effect due to Earth's rotation influences the direction of cyclonic winds
examples: Mountaineers experience difficulty breathing at high altitudes because atmospheric pressure decreases
Barometers detect sudden drops in air pressure which can indicate approaching storms
Cyclones over the Bay of Bengal often bring heavy rainfall and strong winds to coastal areas
Aircraft cabins are pressurized to maintain suitable air pressure for passengers
formulas: Wind movement principle: Air flows from high pressure to low pressure regions
exam_tips: Explain clearly the relationship between temperature, pressure and wind movement
Understand how atmospheric pressure varies with altitude
Study the structure of cyclones including the eye and surrounding high-speed winds
Use real-world examples when explaining storms and cyclone formation
